title: Mulino backs stronger scrutiny of retail MISs
full_text:
Financial Services Minister Daniel Mulino says the government is boosting ASIC’s capability to identify problematic managed investment schemes, while also trying to find a ‘coherent’ way to pass DBFO.
Speaking at the Stockbrokers and Investment Advisers Association (SIAA) conference in Melbourne, Minister Mulino said the federal budget allocated more money to ASIC for it to collect data on flows into MISs as a measure to prevent large-scale collapses.
ASIC announced on 18 May that it would be reviewing reports of MISs with a selection included in ASIC’s review of 25 audit files, alongside listed and unlisted companies and registrable superannuation entities (RSEs) with files being pulled from a variety of sources.
Mulino said: “MISs have a critical role as a collective investment vehicle but they can be problematic for retail investors if they are not transparent or clear or where they lack sufficient diversification.
“We are looking at progressive reforms to strengthen the governance and accountability and regulation of MIS alongside enhancing the supervisory capabilities of ASIC. This is about having sensible guardrails without impeding the contribution MISs have made to our capital markets and economy.”
The budget granted $10.3 million to ASIC to increase MIS oversight and help ensure regulators have the data they need to better identify those concerning structures. A further $7.6 million is being allocated to ASIC, Treasury and the Auditing and Assurance Standards Board to strengthen governance.
“This will put ASIC in a better position to understand the risk profile and have a better sense of who is being targeted for flows into those MISs, allowing a targeted approach by ASIC to examine flows and investors more quickly than is currently possible so that it can prevent large-scale collapses from happening before there are thousands of investors affected.”
Regarding Delivering Better Financial Outcomes (DBFO), Mulino said it has become a “bigger piece of work than imagined” due to the collapse of Shield and First Guardian.
The government’s task now is to work out how it can progress DBFO in a holistic way alongside the Compensation Scheme of Last Resort (CSLR) and Shield and First Guardian.
“Soon after I came into the role, we saw the Shield and First Guardian collapses which raised issues around consumer protection but also raised issues around the sustainability of the CSLR and the burden of CSLR on different sectors.
“So, ultimately, a lot of these issues require attention simultaneously so what I and the government are trying to do is examine those issues in a holistic way. We really are trying to think about these issues and take into account the interdependencies.”
One way it has tackled this is through various discussion papers on topics such as consumer protection, CSLR, lead generation and education standards.
“This has become a bigger piece of overall work than I initially imagined but the opportunity is we will be able to progress on another fronts in a way that is coherent and that is the goal.”
summary: Financial Services Minister Daniel Mulino announced increased funding for ASIC to improve oversight of managed investment schemes (MISs) to protect retail investors and prevent large-scale collapses. The government is also working on broader reforms related to consumer protection and financial outcomes following recent investment scheme failures.
author: Laura Dew
heading: Regulation
page_title: Mulino backs stronger scrutiny of retail MISs - Money Management
key_points: ASIC receives $10.3 million for MIS oversight.
Minister Mulino highlights risks of non-transparent MISs.
Government aims for holistic reforms after recent collapses.
Discussion papers on consumer protection and education standards are being developed.
ticker_line: Government boosts ASIC funding for investment scheme oversight
